← the Atlas

Sleeping terminals

Features· seedling ·accepted ·JUN 20, 2026

A plan for Sleep/Wake. Model the terminal as a sum — Terminal = active or sleeping — so a dormant terminal is a STATE on one record, not a separate thing. Presence (canvas, dock, minimap) reads the union; touching a live field (PTY, agent) narrows to active. Phased — seat the sum (zero behavior change), then sleep/wake, then frozen screenshot, then unify with session-restore.

Contents

  • A terminal is active or sleeping
  • Presence reads the union, liveness narrows
  • The phases
    • Phase 1 — seat the sum (zero behavior change) · shipped
    • Phase 2 — Sleep / Wake
  • Trade-offs & when we’d revisit

You asked for Sleep: leave a Claude Code terminal blocked for days, sleep it (its PTY, xterm, WebGL context, and agent all released — gone, like closing it), and wake it later in place with the agent resumed.

The model is one move: make the terminal a sum — Terminal = active | sleeping. A dormant terminal carries the same persisted base — cwd, git, intent, its canvasLayout slot — so it keeps the canvas position, dock order, and persistence the live terminal had: it lives in the one terminal list the canvas already iterates, just without a PTY. Four phased PRs; the first is a zero-behavior-change foundation.

A terminal is active or sleeping

The fold already lives in the schema. surface.ts splits a terminal’s fields into a persisted base (cwd · git · intent · theme · canvasLayout — survives a restart) and a live overlay (agent · foreground · live-PR · the PTY/xterm/attach handles — “never persisted; a restore must re-derive it”). That partition is active-vs-sleeping, so the sum maps onto bases that already exist — exactly one field is added:

const ActiveTerminalSchema =
  PersistedTerminalFieldsSchema.merge(LiveTerminalFieldsSchema)   // live overlay present
    .extend({ state: z.literal("active") });

const SleepingTerminalSchema =
  PersistedTerminalFieldsSchema                                   // base only — overlay absent by type
    .extend({ state: z.literal("sleeping"), sleptAt: z.number() });

const TerminalMetadataSchema =                                   // the wire / collection shape
  z.discriminatedUnion("state", [ActiveTerminalSchema, SleepingTerminalSchema]);

type Terminal =
  | ({ state: "active" }                    & PersistedTerminalFields & LiveTerminalFields)
  | ({ state: "sleeping"; sleptAt: number } & PersistedTerminalFields);

// Presence reads the union; touching a live field MUST narrow.
const placeTile  = (t: Terminal) => t.canvasLayout;             // both arms — no narrow
const routeInput = (t: Terminal) => {
  if (t.state !== "active") return;                             // compiler-forced narrow
  send(t /* now carries pr · agent · foreground + a live PTY */);
};

sleptAt is the sleeping arm’s analogue of the live overlay — the only new scalar. An active terminal is base + overlay; a sleeping terminal is base + sleptAt. Sleeping is one record whose state flag says whether its PTY is currently spawned.

Presence reads the union, liveness narrows

Putting the discriminant on the terminal buys two structural properties:

  • Type-safe presence vs liveness. A consumer that reads agent/foreground/pr/PTY/xterm must first narrow state === "active"; the compiler refuses a live field on the bare union. There is no live-only list to read from by mistake — so once a sleeping terminal reaches a presence surface (canvas, dock, minimap, arrange, cycle, switcher) it cannot be mis-rendered. Reaching it is a runtime fact, not a type one: a sleeping record only appears if it rides the merged id list the client subscribes to (Phase 2’s load-bearing seam) — the union stops mis-rendering, it does not by itself deliver presence. Input routing resolves inside the active narrow, so a sleeping terminal can be the active/selected/panned-to tile yet is never an input target. The WebGL budget must key on the active arm, so a sleeping terminal holds no WebGL context.
  • One persistence channel. SavedTerminal is already PersistedTerminalFields + id — the sleeping arm’s exact payload minus sleptAt. A restored terminal and a slept terminal are the same on-disk shape, distinguished only by state. Session save emits one merged list — active entries and sleeping records together — so wake is restore-one and sleep is its inverse: each mints a fresh record from the base and retires the predecessor, reusing the proven restore path that already re-spawns from a saved base and resumes the agent.

The phases

Each is one reviewable PR; each leaves master shippable.

PhaseWhat landsWhy separable
1 — Seat the sumAdd state; flip TerminalMetadataSchema to a discriminatedUnion; presence surfaces read the Terminal union off the terminal store; ship with only the active arm constructedZero behavior change — a pure structural move that makes the narrowing seam exist before any sleep logic depends on it
2 — Sleep / WakePopulate the sleeping arm: sleep writes a sleeping record from the base + releases PTY/xterm/agent; wake = restore-one (a fresh active record from the base); the merged id list carries both onto every presence surfaceThe user-facing core; no new component, no second loop
3 — Frozen screenshot bodyCapture just before sleep, write under KOLU_STATE_DIR, serve through a small static image route; the reference rides the recordIsolated surface — one capture + one route
4 — Unify with session-restoreCollapse boot-restore and wake onto one seam (rehydrate a persisted terminal from its base); the session snapshot and sleeping records become one ordered setBehavior-preserving consolidation, landed last

Phase 1 — seat the sum (zero behavior change) · shipped

#1449 . TerminalMetadataSchema flipped from a flat .merge to z.discriminatedUnion("state", …) with only the active arm constructed — the UX stayed pixel-identical while the state === "active" seam every later phase leans on came into being. The server registry holds only the active arm (TerminalProcess.meta: ActiveTerminal); the union flows on the client, where every liveness reader narrows through one activeArm seam, so a live field on a bare terminal no longer compiles. A state.ts migration stamps state: "active" on legacy records and bumps SCHEMA_VERSION — the one sanctioned place the default is supplied; read sites narrow, never coalesce.

Phase 2 — Sleep / Wake

Populate the sleeping arm. The lifecycle is immutable records, not an in-place flip. Sleep writes a fresh sleeping record from the active terminal’s persisted base, then releases the PTY/xterm/agent — persist before kill, never kill-then-pray, so a crash mid-sleep loses nothing. Wake is restore-one — it replays the saved base through the existing restore path, which already spawns a fresh active terminal and resumes the agent; sleep is its exact inverse. Each transition mints a new id and retires the predecessor, so a record is immutable from birth and nothing ever rewrites a live field onto a sleeping one. The server runs each transition as a single op and re-emits the merged id list once, so the client never renders both records — continuity rides the base: the new record carries the same canvasLayout, so the tile keeps its slot and dock order across the swap, and the active selection follows to the successor.

The seam that makes a sleeping terminal real is the wire, not the type. The client subscribes to terminal metadata keyed off the server’s id list, which is active-only today; Phase 2’s load-bearing change is to make that the merged active + sleeping list. The union guarantees a sleeping record can’t be mis-rendered once it arrives — but only the merged list gets it there. Downstream, sleeping terminals are then ordinary cards and rows on the one set the canvas and dock already iterate — focusable/draggable/resizable, selectable, counted toward tileCount so a sleeping-only workspace stays on the canvas — with no new component and no second loop, only a dormant placeholder body (Phase 3 swaps in the frozen frame).

Sleep is manual, Wake is explicit, navigation never wakes. A ☾ Sleep button on the tile title bar, a Sleep/Wake palette command, and a discoverability tip are the only triggers — no global keybind, no auto-sleep. Landing on a sleeping tile (cycle, MRU, dock click, switcher, mobile swipe) focuses it frozen: it becomes the active/selected tile showing its dormant body and an explicit Wake, never an auto-respawn — so the right panel, inspector, and theme for an active-but-sleeping tile fall back to the frozen, no-live-content view plus a Wake call-to-action, not a live attach. Closing a sleeping tile routes through the same close-confirm dialog, reworded to discard sleeping terminal and driven off the still-persisted git/worktree info — it deletes the record (no PTY to kill) and still offers worktree removal. On boot, sleeping records rehydrate as sleeping — the restore card spawns active terminals only and must never wake one.

Non-negotiables for Phase 2

These are requirements, not nice-to-haves — each closes a failure mode that is easy to ship without noticing.

  • Snapshot the top terminal only. A sleeping record is a single terminal — any splits are closed, not frozen. It is rooted by construction — one top-terminal record carrying that terminal’s base — so no orphan or dangling-child record can arise.
  • Tolerate a corrupt persisted record; never let one poison the set. A malformed record is a saved sleeping entry that no longer validates — its base truncated by a crash mid-write, hand-edited, or left by an older build (a missing field, a non-numeric sleptAt, an incomplete base). Validate shape in the schema, then drop a record that fails the cross-field invariant at the read boundary — never a fatal validator on the persisted collection. Sleeping records share the one terminal list, so a single bad entry must not break the load for every other terminal. (See the persisted-schema-stays-tolerant code-police rule.)
  • First-class and visually distinct in every presence surface. A sleeping terminal renders — moonlit, ☾, dimmed — in the canvas, dock, minimap, switcher, and mobile; arrange clusters it with its repo; the cycle traverses it. The distinction must key on state === "sleeping", decoupled from the existing staleness / “parked” vocabulary — else a freshly-slept tile looks like a merely idle one, and the dock’s parked filter (which drops stale rows) silently removes a long-slept terminal from the list. Watch the spots that fold a sleeping terminal into a live default: the dock/switcher agent bucket (it must not land in the No-agent column), the canvas aura, and the minimap marker. And remember omission is a wire fact — verify presence on each surface, don’t assume the sum delivers it.
  • A sleeping tile can be the active tile. Click/select focuses it; input routing narrows to active, so it is never an input target — a type fact, not a runtime guard. Its content panels (right panel, inspector, theme) show the frozen view + Wake, never a live attach.
  • The “by-construction” guards become real edits once sleeping shares the id list. None hold automatically — each is a place the active-only assumption leaks: the WebGL budget must filter the active arm (never grab a context for a frozen tile); session-save must merge active + sleeping (sleeping the last terminal must not clear the session); the boot/restore path must seed sleeping records as sleeping (the restore card spawns active terminals only).
  • An e2e that drives the real failure modes. Seed a malformed record (it must not poison the set), sleep a terminal (it must not vanish — including the last one, which must not clear the session), wake it (it must land a usable live terminal), close a sleeping tile (it must discard through the confirm), and assert a long-slept terminal still shows on each presence surface (not dropped as parked) — driven like a user, not asserted on counts. A green count-only test over a fresh state proves nothing here.

Why the order is the point

Sleep’s machinery is small. The design work is where a dormant terminal lives — Phase 1 answers that with a flag instead of a layer, and everything after is a body, a route, or a consolidation. Small, because the structure finally fits the thing.

Trade-offs & when we’d revisit

  • A migration is mandatory. SavedTerminal is persisted, so adding state needs the state.ts migration above. There is no shipping this as a pure refactor.
  • A wide type-level ripple is the diff. Flipping TerminalMetadataSchema to a discriminatedUnion forces every liveness reader to either stay in the presence subset or add a narrow. That sweep is the win, but it is the bulk of the work.
  • Records are immutable; transitions mint, never mutate. state is fixed at a record’s birth — sleep and wake create a new record and retire the old, so no provider ever rewrites a live field onto a sleeping one (the overlay isn’t on that arm — a type error, which is the point). Re-minting on both transitions buys the win — wake is just restore-one — at the cost of a brief two-record swap the server must sequence (retire, then emit the merged list) so the client never renders both.

When we'd revisit

If a genuine non-terminal canvas kind ever lands — a sticky note, a web embed — introduce a content-kind layer then, designed against two real kinds. Location is structure: the receptacle is justified the day there’s a second thing to put in it, and not before. (See electricity.mdx — the same trap, named three ways.)
